message:
📌 *Dqn*
• DQN uses a neural net to estimate future rewards for actions, picking the one with the highest learned value.
• This works by learning from experience, but value function approximation and exploration are still hard; breaks easily.

📌 *Reranking Systems*
• Reranking refines initial retrieval by meticulously scoring a small candidate set for higher precision.
• It's critical when recall is easy but identifying the *best* items from many similar options is hard.

📌 *Mdp*
• MDPs model sequential decision-making; agent picks actions in states to maximize rewards, with outcomes dependent on current state.
• Critical for optimal control in dynamic systems; breaks down if state observations are incomplete (POMDP needed).

📌 *Isolation Forest*
• Outliers are identified by how few random splits it takes to isolate them in a tree structure.
• This method shines with high-dimensional data, but struggles with tightly clustered anomalies or global density variations.

📌 *Cvar*
• CVaR tells you the average hit you take when losses exceed your VaR threshold.
• It's critical for understanding "how bad" things get, especially when tails are fat.

📌 *Yield Curve*
• The yield curve plots a nation's government bond interest rates across maturities, typically rising with longer terms.
• Its shape reflects market expectations for interest rates and economic growth; an inverted curve often signals recession risk.

📌 *Eigenvalues Eigenvectors*
• Eigenvectors point in directions unchanged by a linear transformation; their eigenvalues are the scaling factors.
• Useful for simplifying complex matrix operations, but works only for square matrices that are diagonalizable.

📌 *Rmse Metric*
• RMSE is your average prediction error, but big mistakes hurt disproportionately, measured in the same units as what you're forecasting.
• Use RMSE for regression where large errors are costly; if RMSE is much higher than MAE, you likely have problematic outliers.

📌 *Drawdown*
• Drawdown is your portfolio's percentage drop from its highest prior value.
• Max Drawdown (MDD) quantifies worst-case loss; Calmar ratio uses it to gauge risk-adjusted returns.

📌 *Perplexity Eval*
• Perplexity quantifies a language model's uncertainty – lower means it's less 'surprised' by the text.
• It's an effective branching factor; high perplexity indicates the model is struggling to predict.

📌 *K Nearest Neighbors*
• KNN classifies new data by finding its 'nearest neighbors' in existing data and using their majority vote.
• It's computationally expensive at prediction and sensitive to feature scaling and the choice of K.

📌 *Map Estimation*
• MAP estimation adds a prior belief about model parameters to MLE, helping to regularize the solution.
• It's crucial when data is sparse, but a strong or misinformed prior can bias the model misleadingly.

📌 *Bleu*
• BLEU scores machine translation quality by comparing n-gram overlap with human references, accounting for word order and completeness.
• It's a standard metric but struggles with semantic meaning, synonyms, or evaluating translations for creative text.

📌 *Speech To Text*
• Speech-to-text converts spoken audio to text, typically using deep learning models like Whisper to process audio features.
• Accuracy is measured by Word Error Rate (WER); robust models handle various languages and accents, but struggle with noisy data or specific domain jargon.

📌 *Vision Language Models*
• VLMs combine image and text understanding, converting visual content into "visual tokens" processed by a large language model.
• They excel at cross-modal tasks like visual QA but can struggle with fine-grained visual details or complex spatial reasoning.

📌 *Law Of Large Numbers*
• The more data points you collect, the closer your sample average gets to the actual underlying average.
• Crucial for statistical inference and risk modeling; breaks down if observations aren't independent or identically distributed.

📌 *Llm Eval Frameworks*
• LLM evaluation frameworks automate quality checks for your AI app using other LLMs as judges.
• They're crucial for scaling RAG quality; without them, manual review becomes a crippling bottleneck.

📌 *Causal Lm*
• Causal LMs predict the next word given everything observed so far, left-to-right, using a masked attention mechanism.
• This architecture for GPT-like models excels at text generation, but can't "see" future context even when available.

📌 *Greeks*
• Greeks quantify options sensitivity: Delta for price, Gamma for delta's change, Theta for time decay, Vega for volatility.
• Delta neutrality eliminates stock price risk, but Gamma exposes you to cost of rebalancing as stock moves.

📌 *Neural Architecture Search*
• NAS finds optimal neural network designs by searching architectures, replacing manual trial and error.
• bilevel optimization (DARTS) is key to solving this complex search, but can suffer from architecture collapse.

📌 *Taylor Series*
• Taylor series approximates functions locally with polynomials; effectively, it's a "zoomed-in" polynomial representation.
• It matters for optimization (Newton's method), hedging derivatives, and understanding model sensitivities, but breaks down far from the expansion point.

📌 *Bootstrap*
• Bootstrap replays your experiment many times by drawing samples with replacement to estimate a statistic's distribution.
• It provides robust confidence intervals without strong distributional assumptions, but struggles with extreme tail events or tiny datasets.

📌 *Gru*
• GRU simplifies LSTMs by combining gates and states, using one update gate (z_t) and one reset gate (r_t) for memory.
• This design offers similar performance with ~25% fewer parameters, suitable when computational efficiency is critical.

📌 *Swaps*
• A swap means you bet on fixed vs. floating rates; only interest difference changes hands, no principal.
• Fixed-payer swaps shorten portfolio duration, receive-fixed extends it – critical for hedging.

📌 *Feature Stores*
• A feature store centralizes ML feature definitions and stores their values for consistent access.
• Without one, training data often differs from serving data, causing production model degradation.

📌 *Rouge*
• ROUGE scores quantify how much of the human-written summary your model successfully captured, focusing on recall.
• Useful for evaluating text summarisation; however, it struggles with paraphrasing and capturing semantic similarity beyond exact word matches.

📌 *Correlation Risk*
•  Correlation risk is when asset relationships change, often spiking to 1 when you need diversification most.
•  Historically negative equity-bond correlation flipped with recent inflation; diversification fails in crises.

📌 *Cosine Annealing*
• Cosine annealing schedules learning rates using a cosine curve, smoothly decreasing from high to low.
• This smooth transition avoids abrupt drops of step decay, offering better convergence without jarring the optimizer.

📌 *Mode*
• The mode identifies the most common observation in a dataset.
• It's the only central tendency for categorical data, but can be non-unique (multi-modal) or non-existent in continuous data.

📌 *Cross Validation*
• Train your model multiple times on different data subsets, testing each time on unseen parts.
• This gives a more reliable estimate of real-world performance than a single train/validation split.

📌 *Bert Architecture*
• BERT's full bidirectional attention allows deep contextual understanding by seeing all tokens, unlike models that only look left.
• This bidirectional view excels at tasks like text classification or QA, but makes it fundamentally unsuitable for generating new text autoregressively.

📌 *Real Assets*
• Real assets are tangible things generating income directly, like property or infrastructure, not financial claims.
• They diversify portfolios due to physical supply/demand, but reported valuations often understate true volatility.

📌 *Evaluation Under Imbalance*
• When data is imbalanced, accuracy gives a false sense of security; it's a terrible metric.
• Focus on metrics like PR-AUC or F1-score for the minority class, which truly assess performance.

📌 *Multi Armed Bandit*
• MAB balances trying new options (exploration) with sticking to known good ones (exploitation) to maximize returns.
• It's foundational for balancing quick wins with discovering better long-term strategies, e.g., A/B testing or portfolio optimization.

📌 *Adagrad*
• Adagrad scales learning rates inversely to the square root of past gradients for each parameter.
• This adapts learning to parameter volatility but the decay to a zero learning rate is a major drawback.

📌 *Vectors Matrices*
• Vectors are ordered lists of numbers pointing in a specific direction; matrices are grids that transform these vectors.
• This transformation is fundamental to modeling data movement, like price fluctuations or portfolio rebalancing.
